title: Determining universal spectra from probability distributions
authors: Charles Kacir, Joseph Moscoso, Amy Nicholson, Thomas R. Richardson, Cade Rodgers
abstract: The probability distribution of a two-particle correlation function computed over background auxiliary field configurations, used to generate the interactions, has been shown to inform about the spectra of universal $n$-body clusters [1]. Here, we utilize two approaches, a numerical lattice computation and an analytic expansion in the limit of large numbers of identical species, in an attempt to refine the initial predictions. Exploratory calculations in these directions are presented, and future investigations laid out.
summary: The paper develops methods to extract universal spectral information about strongly interacting quantum matter by analyzing the probability distributions of two-particle correlation functions. It combines numerical lattice techniques with analytic large-$N$ expansions, showing that ratios of cluster energies provide scale-independent predictions. This methodology is crucial for understanding physics in regimes like unitarity where simple perturbative approaches fail.
main_problem: The central goal is determining universal spectral information about interacting quantum systems by analyzing probability distributions derived from two-particle correlation functions.
main_result: The raw moments of the distribution are shown to be equivalent to the energies of $n$-body clusters, suggesting that ratios of these energies can yield cutoff-independent predictions for universal physics.
method: The authors employ both numerical lattice computations and analytic expansions in the large-$N$ limit, utilizing cumulant expansions ($\kappa_n$) of the logarithm of correlation functions to extract spectral information.
model_or_system: The study concerns strongly-interacting quantum matter fields (fermions $\psi$) interacting via bosonic auxiliary fields ($\phi$), relevant to theories like QCD and low-energy EFTs. The analysis focuses on two-fermion correlation functions $C_2(	au)$ in the context of non-relativistic, two-component fermions.
key_observables: Two-particle correlation function ($C_2(	au)$), cumulants ($\kappa_n$), and ratios of cluster energies ($E_{2n}/E_4$).
important_parameters: Large Euclidean time ($	au 	o \infty$), the limit of large number of species ($N 	o \infty$), and parameters related to unitarity.
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes that the distribution approaches a log-normal form, which simplifies the extraction of spectral information from moments. A key assumption is that universal physics can be extracted by removing non-universal cutoff effects via ratios.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares direct calculations of $n$-body energies against log-normal predictions for the $N=2$ correlator; Figure 2 shows cumulants plateauing, suggesting convergence to a stable spectral regime; Figure 3 illustrates differences in correlation function derivations between BCS and density channels.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the link between moments and cluster energies. It then develops two complementary theoretical frameworks—a lattice approach and an analytic large-$N$ expansion (comparing 'density' vs. 'BCS' channels)—to calculate correlation functions, ultimately deriving conditions for universal spectral determination.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it tackles the difficult problem of extracting fundamental, scale-invariant physics from complex many-body correlators in strongly interacting systems, a core challenge in modern quantum field theory and AMO research involving ultracold atomic gases near Feshbach resonances.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Energy of a universal N-body cluster, EN, relative to the 4-body energy, E4, as a function of N. Results assuming a log-normal distribution for the N = 2 correlator are shown in green. Data and other analytic predictions are taken from: von Stecher 2010 [12], Gattobigio 2014 [13], Yan & Blume 2015 [14], Kievsky 2017 [15], Carlson 2017 [16].
FIG. 2. Contributions to the third cumulant, κ3, of ln (M[ϕ, τ])2, as a function of τ (top) and 1/L (bottom). The band on the lower figure corresponds to a linear extrapolation in 1/L.

FIG. 3. Graphs contributing to the two-particle correla- tion function, Eq. 3.8. Fermionic propagators are denoted by solid lines with arrows following the progression of time, while bosonic auxiliary field contributions are dashed.
